title: Expressing strong disapproval of the President's announcement to withdraw the United States from the Paris Agreement.
plain_summary: This bill is about Congress disagreeing with President Trump's decision to pull the United States out of the Paris Agreement, which is a deal between countries to fight climate change. The bill explains that climate change is causing serious problems and costing a lot of money, and that the Paris Agreement is important for fixing these issues. It also says that most Americans and many states want the US to stay in the agreement. Congress is asking the President to change his mind and wants the country to be a leader in stopping climate change.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses strong disapproval of the President's announcement to withdraw the United States from the Paris Agreement.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: The year two thousand twenty four was the hottest year ever recorded. The previous ten years were the ten hottest years recorded since eighteen fifty. Climate change around the world threatens all Americans' health, wealth, and safety. Climate change threatens the United States public health, national economy, national security, and the world we will leave to our children. According to the two thousand twenty three Fifth National Climate Assessment, harmful impacts from more frequent and severe extreme weather are increasing across the country. These include increases in heat-related illnesses and death, costlier storm damages, longer droughts that reduce farm productivity and strain water systems, and larger, more severe wildfires that threaten homes and make air quality worse. According to the same assessment, extreme weather events cost the United States close to one hundred fifty billion dollars each year. This is a conservative estimate that does not account for loss of life, healthcare-related costs, or damages to natural services. Between two thousand eighteen and two thousand twenty two, eighty nine weather and climate disasters cost the United States one billion dollars or more each. These included four droughts, six floods, fifty two severe storms, eighteen tropical cyclones, five wildfires, and four winter storm events. The most vulnerable people among us, including children, elderly people, low-income individuals, and those with underlying health conditions, face even greater health risks because of climate change. The National Intelligence Council's two thousand twenty one report on climate change stated that risks to United States national security interests through two thousand forty will increase as countries respond to the worsening physical effects of climate change. Global temperatures will most likely surpass the Paris Agreement goal of one and a half degrees Celsius by around two thousand thirty. The physical effects are projected to continue getting worse. On May twenty seven, two thousand twenty one, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff General Mark Milley told the House Appropriations Defense Subcommittee that climate change presents a growing threat to United States national security interests and defense objectives. The harmful impacts of climate change are already being felt across the Joint Force through increased operational demands, harmful impacts on military installations, and new requirements for equipment and formations able to operate in a world defined by climate change. Climate change also contributes to regional instability. The Paris Agreement is an international accord that aims to limit the increase in global temperatures to less than two degrees Celsius. It urges efforts to limit the increase to one and a half degrees Celsius by twenty one hundred in order to avoid the most disastrous impacts of climate change. The Paris Agreement was adopted on December twelve, two thousand fifteen, opened for signature on April twenty two, two thousand sixteen, and entered into force on November four, two thousand sixteen. One hundred ninety five parties, including the largest emitters of carbon pollution like China, India, and the European Union, have signed the Paris Agreement. On January twenty, two thousand twenty five, President Trump announced his intention to withdraw the United States from the Paris Agreement. During his first term in office on June one, two thousand seventeen, President Trump withdrew the United States from the Paris Agreement. On November four, two thousand twenty, the United States formally withdrew from the Paris Agreement. United States withdrawal from the Paris Agreement breaks our commitment to the global community to fulfill our responsibility as a party to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change. As a major emitter of carbon pollution, we committed to reduce our emissions. The United States exit from the Paris Agreement will give up leadership on clean energy technologies and the jobs they create to China and other nations. If the United States again withdraws from the Paris Agreement, it will join Iran, Libya, and Yemen as the only nation states not participating in the agreement. President Biden brought the United States back into the Paris Agreement on February nineteen, two thousand twenty one. Since rejoining the Paris Agreement, the United States passed important climate legislation including the Inflation Reduction Act, Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and CHIPS and Science Act. Together these have created four hundred six thousand new jobs and four hundred twenty two billion dollars in private investments as of January two thousand twenty five. They put the United States on track to achieve approximately forty percent carbon dioxide emissions reductions. This brings the United States closer to fulfilling its commitment under the Paris Agreement to achieve fifty percent reductions by two thousand thirty. The United States is rapidly bringing critical supply chains back to America and encouraging a resurgence of investments in domestic manufacturing for innovative technologies. This has resulted in the manufacturing sector contribution to United States gross domestic product reaching an all time high. The United States can continue to lead the world in innovation and manufacturing clean energy technologies. This creates good paying jobs, modernizes the energy grid, and grows new companies that will be the titans of a new clean energy economy. According to research published in April two thousand twenty four in the European Economic Review, non participation of the United States would eliminate more than a third of the world emissions reduction. A potential non participation of China lowers the world emission reduction by twenty four point one percent. In terms of welfare, the overwhelming majority of countries gain from implementing the Paris Agreement. Most countries have only very little to gain from deciding not to participate on their own. Leaders of the world's religious communities recognize the grave threat to humanity posed by climate change. They recognize our moral obligation to protect the Earth and its people. They have publicly called upon politicians, business leaders, and the faithful to take action to address climate change. On October ten, two thousand twenty four, the Alliance of CEO Climate Leaders, representing four trillion dollars in revenues and twelve million employees, wrote an open letter reiterating the need to enhance collaboration to deliver on the Paris Agreement goals. A group of twenty two States, including Arizona, California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii, Illinois, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Vermont, Washington, Wisconsin, and the territories of Puerto Rico and Guam, have all joined the United States Climate Alliance. This is a bipartisan group of States committed to upholding the goals of the Paris Agreement. According to data from the two thousand twenty three Chicago Council Survey, conducted in September two thousand twenty three, the American public broadly supports United States participation in international agreements. Sixty eight percent of Americans support the Paris Agreement. Millions of Americans have made their voices heard in support of the Paris Agreement and the United States upholding its commitments to the international community to reduce carbon pollution for the benefit of good paying jobs, families, and the environment now and in future generations.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives strongly disapproves of the President's announcement to withdraw the United States from the Paris Agreement. The House commends the group of States, cities, colleges and universities, businesses, investors, and individuals who have publicly expressed their support for the Paris Agreement. The House urges the President to reverse his decision and maintain United States participation in the Paris Agreement. The House urges Congress to prioritize the United States global leadership on addressing climate change.
